consensus_findings.description: The /chat SSE payloads are serialized with Pydantic's default field names and include null upstream AG-UI fields, so they do not validate against contracts/events/ag-ui-events.schema.json. src/assistant/web/routes.py:47 and :58 call model_dump_json() without by_alias=True or exclude_none=True; a RunStartedEvent serializes as thread_id/run_id plus timestamp/raw_event/parent_run_id/input null fields, while the contract requires threadId/runId and additionalProperties=false. Existing web and smoke tests only assert the type field, so this contract break is not caught.

The production web agent factory closes the httpx.AsyncClient that discovered HTTP tools before the agent can use those tools. discover_tools explicitly documents that returned tools close over the caller-owned client and require it to live as long as the registry, but src/assistant/web/app.py:69-75 creates the client inside an async with and then passes closed-client tools into harness.create_agent. Any /chat turn that invokes a discovered HTTP tool will fail at runtime with a closed client.
The AG-UI mapper fails to include the mandatory 'role' field when yielding TextMessageStartEvent.

Both SDK harnesses use `except BaseException` followed by a `yield RunFinished(error=type(exc).__name__)` from inside the except block (deep_agents.py:198-207, ms_agent_fw.py:330-337 and 387-394). This is a Python async-generator anti-pattern: `BaseException` includes `GeneratorExit`, `asyncio.CancelledError`, `KeyboardInterrupt`, and `SystemExit`. Yielding while handling `GeneratorExit` raises `RuntimeError('asynchronous generator ignored GeneratorExit')`; cancellation paths get mislabeled as 'errors' and propagate a synthesized terminal event the spec did not ask for. The route's IMPL_ITERATE fix #3 (`aclosing(harness.astream_invoke(...))`) drives exactly this finalization path on every client disconnect — the spec scenario 'Client disconnect during streaming cancels the harness' is the documented trigger. Because the disconnect test (tests/web/test_app.py:328) uses `_make_text_harness()` (a fake that just yields a fixed event list, with no `try/except BaseException` mirror of the real harness), the bug is uncovered by CI. The same gap exists in tests/harnesses/test_deep_agents_astream.py and test_ms_agent_fw_astream.py — they exercise Phase 1/2 via `_make_error_stream` (a deliberate Exception raise), never via `aclose()`/`GeneratorExit`.

The client-disconnect cleanup is still incomplete because @traced_harness wraps astream_invoke in a second async generator that does not close the inner harness generator when the wrapper is aclose()d. routes.py correctly uses contextlib.aclosing on harness.astream_invoke(...), but that closes only the traced wrapper; decorators.py:246-248 iterates the inner fn(...) generator without aclosing/finally, so a mid-stream disconnect can leave the underlying LangGraph or MSAF stream unclosed.
Harnesses synthesize different UUIDs for ToolCallStart and ToolCallEnd when the upstream SDK ID is missing, breaking call bracketing.

tests/cli/test_serve.py::test_serve_uses_default_role_when_r_omitted asserts `make_app_calls[0]['role'] in ('chief_of_staff', 'assistant')` — accepting either of two unrelated values. This is a tautological assertion: it does not verify the `serve` command actually pulled `default_role` off the persona config, only that the value is one of two strings. If the fallback logic in cli.py:327-332 ever regressed to e.g. hard-coding 'assistant' or reading a wrong attribute, the test would still pass. The test should look up the fixture persona's actual `default_role` value (or, better, monkeypatch the loaded PersonaConfig to set a sentinel default_role like 'sentinel-default-role-xyz' and assert exact equality) so the fallback path is genuinely exercised.
Harnesses serialize tool arguments into a single ToolCallArgs event without checking the 1MB limit defined in the HarnessEvent schema.
The AG-UI mapper does not close open text messages when a tool call starts, leading to interleaved protocol events that may confuse some clients.
DeepAgentsHarness overwrites its thread_id in create_agent, violating the stability requirement for the adapter's lifetime.

`assistant serve` constructs the harness twice. cli.py:341 calls `_create_harness(pc, rc, harness)` to validate the harness type (host-vs-SDK rejection on lines 345-351), then make_app() at cli.py:361 internally calls `create_harness(pc, rc, harness_name)` again at app.py:123. The first instance is discarded immediately. For deep_agents this is harmless; for harnesses with non-trivial __init__ side effects (e.g. seeding ChatClient credentials, opening a checkpointer) it doubles the cost and could surface ordering surprises if a future harness logs at construction time. The same host-vs-SDK rejection is enforced inside make_app (app.py:124-128), so the CLI-side rejection is redundant.

The non-loopback warning in cli.py:353 uses `if host != '127.0.0.1' and not host.startswith('127.')`. This misses two common loopback spellings: `::1` (IPv6 loopback — fires a 'binding to non-loopback host' warning even though `::1` IS loopback) and `localhost` (resolves to loopback on every default-configured host). The false positive on `::1` is the more interesting case: an operator binding `--host ::1` for IPv6-only is told they are exposing the server, which is wrong and may push them to weaker bindings. Conversely `localhost` triggering the warning is technically correct on locked-down hosts where `/etc/hosts` maps localhost to a non-loopback address, but the same warning will fire on every standard machine where it shouldn't.
The @traced_harness decorator records GeneratorExit (client disconnect) as an error in the LLM trace metadata.
